items.title: Risk factors in AMD
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors in AMD

  • Age >50 years is the main factor; Risk increases exponentially after age 75.
  • Smoking doubles the risk (OR 2-3) and is associated with more rapid progression to advanced forms.
  • Genetics: polymorphisms in CFH (complement factor H) and ARMS2/HTRA1 confer greater susceptibility; Family history of AMD increases the risk 3-4 times.
  • Others: high blood pressure, obesity, cumulative sun exposure.

items.title: AMD Classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification of AMD

  • Dry AMD (atrophic): represents 85-90% of cases; It presents with thinning of the outer retina, drusen and progressive geographic atrophy.
  • Wet AMD (neovascular): 10-15% but causes 90% of severe visual loss; characterized by choroidal neovascularization (CNV) with exudation and hemorrhage.
  • Dry AMD can progress to wet; the presence of confluent soft drusen and focal hyperpigmentation are predictors of conversion.

items.foco: Key finding in dry AMD: drusen and geographic atrophy
items.title: Findings in dry AMD
items.contentHtml:
Findings in dry AMD

  • Drusen: extracellular deposits between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and Bruch's membrane; They are classified as hard (small, <63 µm, lower risk) and soft (>125 µm, poorly defined borders, higher risk of progression).
  • Geographic atrophy: well-defined loss of the RPE and photoreceptors; On retinography it is observed as areas of hypopigmentation with visualization of choroidal vessels.
  • Geographic atrophy is a marker of advanced dry AMD and is associated with progressive central scotoma.

items.foco: Key finding in wet AMD: choroidal neovascularization
items.title: Findings in wet AMD
items.contentHtml:
Findings in wet AMD

  • Choroidal neovascularization (CNV): abnormal growth of vessels from the choroid into the subretinal space or sub-RPE.
  • On fluorescein angiography (FAG) it is classified into classical CNV (early well-defined leakage) and occult CNV (late leakage) defined).
  • Associated findings: detachment of the retinal pigment epithelium (PED), lipid exudation, subretinal hemorrhage and fibrovascular membrane.

items.foco: Main symptom: metamorphopsia and central scotoma
items.title: Main symptoms
items.contentHtml:
Main symptoms

  • Metamorphopsia: distortion of straight lines (wavy, twisted); It is the earliest and most sensitive symptom of wet AMD.
  • Central scotoma: loss of central vision, initially relative and then absolute; It affects reading and facial recognition.
  • In advanced dry AMD (geographic atrophy) visual loss is slower and more progressive; in wet AMD the onset can be acute or subacute (days-weeks).
  • The Amsler grid detects metamorphopsia and scotomas; It is used as home self-monitoring.

items.foco: Initial diagnostic test: retinography and Amsler grid
items.title: Initial diagnostic tests
items.contentHtml:
Initial diagnostic tests

  • Retinography (fundus photography): documents drusen, geographic atrophy, exudates, hemorrhages and PED.
  • Amsler grid: simple screening test; the patient reports distortion or blurred areas; useful for home monitoring.
  • Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is a first-line complementary in current practice (non-invasive, shows intra/subretinal fluid).
  • Retinography with autofluorescence (FAF) can detect early geographic atrophy.

items.title: Diagnostic gold standard
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic gold standard

  • Fluorescein angiography (FAG): gold standard to identify and classify choroidal neovascularization.
  • It allows differentiation of classic CNV (well-defined early leak) from occult CNV (poorly defined late leak) and CNV mixed.
  • Indications: confirm wet AMD when suspected clinically or on OCT with liquid; guide anti-VEGF treatment.
  • Indocyanine green (ICG) angiography is useful for occult CNV or choroidal polyps.

items.foco: Treatment for wet AMD: intravitreal anti-VEGF
items.title: Wet AMD treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment of wet AMD

  • First line: intravitreal anti-VEGF; drugs: ranibizumab (Lucentis) 0.5 mg, aflibercept (Eylea) 2 mg, bevacizumab (Avastin) 1.25 mg (off-label but effective).
  • Guidelines: monthly dose or treat-and-extend regimen; objective: resolve intra/subretinal fluid and improve visual acuity.
  • Contraindications: active ocular infection, severe intraocular inflammation, drug allergy.
  • Adverse effects: endophthalmitis (0.02-0.05% per injection), retinal detachment, vitreous hemorrhage.

items.foco: Growth factor involved: VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor)
items.title: Growth factor involved
items.contentHtml:
Growth factor involved

  • VEGF (vascular endothelial growth factor): especially the VEGF-A isoform is the main mediator of choroidal neovascularization in wet AMD.
  • It is produced by RPE cells in response to hypoxia, oxidative stress and inflammation; induces vascular permeability and endothelial proliferation.
  • Blockade of VEGF with monoclonal antibodies (ranibizumab, bevacizumab) or fusion proteins (aflibercept) constitutes the basis of treatment.
  • New drugs: faricimab (anti-VEGF-A and anti-Ang-2) and brolucizumab (high scFv fragment affinity).

items.foco: Supplement indicated in dry AMD: AREDS2 formula (lutein, zeaxanthin)
items.title: Supplement in dry DMAE
items.contentHtml:
Supplement in dry AMD

  • AREDS2 formula: indicated in intermediate or advanced dry AMD with large drusen (>125 µm) or non-central geographic atrophy.
  • Composition: lutein 10 mg, zeaxanthin 2 mg, vitamin C 500 mg, vitamin E 400 IU, zinc oxide 80 mg, cupric oxide 2 mg.
  • The AREDS2 formula eliminated beta-carotene due to the risk of lung cancer in smokers; It does not replace anti-VEGF treatment in wet AMD.
  • It is not indicated in early AMD (only small drusen) or in patients without macular involvement.

items.foco: Serious complication: retinal pigment epithelium detachment
items.title: Serious complication
items.contentHtml:
Serious complication

  • Detachment of the retinal pigment epithelium (PED): separation between the RPE and Bruch's membrane, it can be serous, fibrovascular or hemorrhagic.
  • The PED is a common complication in wet AMD and is associated with a worse visual prognosis and a greater need for anti-VEGF injections.
  • On OCT it is observed as a elevated RPE with hyporeflective (serous) or hyperreflective (fibrovascular) content.
  • Hemorrhagic PED can mimic choroidal melanoma; requires angiography for differential diagnosis.

items.foco: Difference with hard vs soft drusen: soft ones are at greater risk
items.title: Hard vs soft drusen
items.contentHtml:
Hard vs soft drusen

  • Hard drusen: <63 µm, sharp edges, yellowish color; they are not associated with significant progression to advanced AMD.
  • Soft drusen: >125 µm, poorly defined borders, confluent; indicate high risk of progression to wet AMD or geographic atrophy.
  • The soft drusen contain a greater amount of lipids and amorphous material; its confluence forms drusenoid DEP.
  • The presence of bilateral and large soft drusen is a criterion for starting AREDS2 formula.
